attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic at 13 weeks gestation. She has experienced two previous pregnancy losses at 16 and 18 weeks gestation. She has had a LLETZ procedure for CIN 3 five years ago but has had normal cervical screening tests following. The only medication she takes are pregnancy supplements. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits.

Her combined first trimester screening test shows low risk for chromosomal abnormalities. Transvaginal ultrasound reveals a viable singleton pregnancy with appropriate growth. The cervical length measures 15 mm (25-50 mm).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Expectant management with serial ultrasound monitoring
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a history highly suggestive of cervical insufficiency, given her two previous mid-trimester losses and current short cervical length. LLETZ may have increased the risk of cervical insufficiency in this case. The cervical length of 15 mm is significantly below the normal range of 25-50 mm for this gestation.

Cervical cerclage and vaginal progesterone is the most appropriate management. The combination of previous mid-trimester losses and current short cervix makes her an excellent candidate for prophylactic cervical cerclage. The addition of vaginal progesterone provides additional benefit in preventing preterm birth.

Bed rest and pelvic rest have not been shown to prevent preterm birth or pregnancy loss in women with cervical insufficiency. This management strategy lacks evidence to support its use.

Aspirin would not address the underlying cervical insufficiency. While aspirin may be indicated in pregnancy for other conditions like pre-eclampsia prevention, it has no role in preventing cervical insufficiency or preterm birth.

Expectant management with serial ultrasound monitoring would be inadequate given her high-risk status and already demonstrated short cervix. This approach would risk another pregnancy loss.

Vaginal progesterone supplementation alone would be insufficient given her history of recurrent losses and very short cervical length. While progesterone is beneficial, it should be combined with cerclage in this high-risk scenario.

The optimal timing for cervical cerclage is typically between 12-14 weeks gestation, making this presentation ideal for intervention. The procedure should be performed by an experienced operator, and the patient should be counselled about the risks including infection, bleeding, and membrane rupture.
learningPoints: Women with a history of recurrent late pregnancy loss and short cervix should be offered cervical cerclage.
Cervical length less than 25mm in the first trimester is considered short and warrants intervention in high-risk women.
Vaginal progesterone supplementation is recommended alongside cervical cerclage in cases of cervical insufficiency.
references: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE). Preterm labour and birth [Internet]. London: NICE; 2015 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ng25

Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Measurement of cervical length for prediction of preterm birth [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2023 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Measurement-Cervical-Length-Preterm-Birth.pdf

Queensland Health. Guideline for premature rupture of membranes at term [Internet]. Brisbane: Queensland Health; 2021 [cited 2024 June 7]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0019/140149/g-ptl.pdf
